Musical artist

**Lee Aaron** (born **Karen Lynn Greening**; July 21, 1962) is a Canadian [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") singer.[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinHR-5) She had several hits in the 1980s and early 1990s, such as "Metal Queen", "Whatcha Do to My Body", and "Sex with Love".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lee_Aaron&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Aaron was born as Karen Lynn Greening in [Belleville, Ontario](/wiki/Belleville,_Ontario "Belleville, Ontario"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) and began singing in school musicals at the age of five. She attended [high school](/wiki/High_school "High school") in [Brampton, Ontario](/wiki/Brampton,_Ontario "Brampton, Ontario").

At age seventeen, Aaron was in a car accident. No surgery was required, but she did suffer a broken nose and badly bruised her face. However, years later *Canadian Musician Magazine* mistakenly embellished the incident into Aaron requiring complete facial reconstruction.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-jam-9) The magazine printed a retraction in the following month's issue.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lee_Aaron&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

After singing in a music production when she was fifteen years old, she was asked to join a local rock group, which the members including Karen would name "Lee Aaron". The members placed random names into a hat and drawn by the young musicians. [[11]](#cite_note-11) She sang, played [alto saxophone](/wiki/Alto_saxophone "Alto saxophone") and [keyboards](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument") in this first incarnation of the band, and took on the stage name of Lee Aaron.

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lee_Aaron&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1980s")]

Aaron recorded her debut album, *[The Lee Aaron Project](/wiki/The_Lee_Aaron_Project "The Lee Aaron Project")*, in 1982 on Freedom Records;[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinHR-5) it was later reissued on Attic. The album featured well-known musicians of the [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") music scene, including members of [Moxy](/wiki/Moxy_(band) "Moxy (band)"), Rif Raf3 (Q107 Homegrown Vol 4 / Lee Aaron Project band), [Santers](/w/index.php?title=Santers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Santers (page does not exist)"), and [Triumph](/wiki/Triumph_(band) "Triumph (band)")'s [Rik Emmett](/wiki/Rik_Emmett "Rik Emmett").[[12]](#cite_note-doole-12) Although the album was available in the UK only as an import, Aaron was invited to appear later that year at the [Reading Festival](/wiki/Reading_Festival "Reading Festival").

In late 1982, Aaron flew to [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and posed topless for the men's magazine *[OUI](/wiki/Oui_Magazine "Oui Magazine")*.[[9]](#cite_note-jam-9) The magazine's March 1983 issue featured Aaron on the cover and in an interview. Aaron later concluded that posing for the magazine damaged her musical credibility;[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinHR-5) she regretted the decision and assigned blame for it to pressure from her manager.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

1984's recording of the album *[Metal Queen](/wiki/Metal_Queen "Metal Queen")* resulted in a multi-album deal with [Attic Records](/wiki/Attic_Records "Attic Records").[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinHR-5) During the recording of *Metal Queen*, guitarist John Albani (ex-Wrabit)[[14]](#cite_note-14) joined the band and he and Aaron formed a solid songwriting partnership that lasted eleven years.[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinHR-5)

Between 1984 and 1992, Aaron toured extensively through Europe and made appearances in Japan and the United States. She released six albums on Attic Records in Canada and international releases in the United Kingdom, Germany, Austria, Switzerland, [Benelux](/wiki/Benelux "Benelux"), Italy, [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), Australia and Japan. She won three [Toronto Music Awards](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_Music_Awards&action=edit&redlink=1 "Toronto Music Awards (page does not exist)") for Best Female Vocalist and was nominated for [Juno](/wiki/Juno_Award "Juno Award") awards sixteen times.[[15]](#cite_note-15) She amassed zero wins. [[16]](#cite_note-16) Her 1985 album *[Call Of The Wild](/wiki/Call_of_the_Wild_(Lee_Aaron_album) "Call of the Wild (Lee Aaron album)")* featured her well-known rock ballad "Barely Holdin On".

Her most commercially successful album was *[Bodyrock](/wiki/Bodyrock_(album) "Bodyrock (album)")*, released in 1989.[[5]](#cite_note-LarkinHR-5) The album included several hit songs, including "Whatcha Do to My Body", and was certified double platinum in Canada.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lee_Aaron&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1990s")]

In 1992, Aaron left Attic Records to start her label, Hip Chic Music, and released two more albums. On 1994's *[Emotional Rain](/wiki/Emotional_Rain "Emotional Rain")* (distributed by [A & M Canada](/wiki/A%26M_Records "A&M Records")), Aaron recorded with Don Short and [Don Binns](/w/index.php?title=Don_Binns&action=edit&redlink=1 "Don Binns (page does not exist)") ([Sons of Freedom](/wiki/Sons_of_Freedom_(band) "Sons of Freedom (band)")), [Reeves Gabrels](/wiki/Reeves_Gabrels "Reeves Gabrels") ([David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie")'s [Tin Machine](/wiki/Tin_Machine "Tin Machine")), and [Knox Chandler](/wiki/Knox_Chandler "Knox Chandler") ([the Psychedelic Furs](/wiki/The_Psychedelic_Furs "The Psychedelic Furs")).

1995's *[2preciious](/wiki/2preciious "2preciious")* was a project record written with members of Sons of Freedom, in which she dropped the name "Lee Aaron" entirely and used her real name, Karen. Neither of these recordings was commercially successful, and shortly afterwards, the Sons broke up.[[18]](#cite_note-Inc.2000-18)

Aaron studied acting for a year before beginning to perform jazz and blues in Toronto with Dennis Ziebart.[[18]](#cite_note-Inc.2000-18)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lee_Aaron&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/2017_Lee_Aaron_-_by_2eight_-_DSC1612.jpg/250px-2017_Lee_Aaron_-_by_2eight_-_DSC1612.jpg)](/wiki/File:2017_Lee_Aaron_-_by_2eight_-_DSC1612.jpg)

Aaron in 2017

In 2000, Aaron released a jazz album, *Slick Chick*, on her imprint, Barking Dog Music, with distribution by Fusion 3.[[18]](#cite_note-Inc.2000-18) She performed extensively in support of this release, showcasing at The Top of the Senator in Toronto and various jazz festivals across Canada and Europe.

In 2002, Aaron was approached to audition with the Modern Baroque Opera Company. She was cast and appeared that year in an ALCAN Performing Arts Award-winning production called *101 Songs for the Marquis De Sade*.

Her eleventh album, *Beautiful Things*, a pop-jazz hybrid, was released in 2004.

Aaron continued to play rock and selected jazz shows; in 2011 she made her first appearance in Sweden at the [Sweden Rock Festival](/wiki/Sweden_Rock_Festival "Sweden Rock Festival").[[19]](#cite_note-srf-19)

In March, 2016, Lee Aaron released her first all-rock album in 20 years. Self-financed and released on her own Big Sister Records, the new album was entitled *Fire And Gasoline*.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Later that year she was inducted into the Brampton Arts Walk Of Fame.[[12]](#cite_note-doole-12) She then went on to appear at the Rockingham 2016 melodic/hard rock festival in Nottingham, United Kingdom.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 2017, Lee Aaron played a number of shows in Germany as well as an appearance at Bang Your Head Festivals and two separate gigs in London and Wolverhampton.[[22]](#cite_note-Lee_Aaron_London_gig_review-22)

Lee Aaron performing at the Kitchener Blues Festival
